label: ai
topic: Satirical argument that university student government should be replaced with a monarchy
text:
Every spring, like clockwork, our campus descends into the particular brand of performative democracy that is the student government election. Candidates materialise from the ether with platforms so ambitious they border on fantasy, promising everything from extended library hours to the abolition of parking fees, as though the student senate controls municipal infrastructure. Voter turnout hovers around twelve percent, which means that the person who will spend the next year claiming to represent ten thousand undergraduates was chosen by roughly the same number of people who attend a mid-semester poetry reading.

The system, in short, is broken. It has always been broken. The founders of our student government modelled it on representative democracy, which was their first mistake, because representative democracy requires representatives who are, in some meaningful sense, representative. Our candidates are invariably drawn from the same narrow band of campus operatives who have been running for things since orientation week and whose primary qualification is an enthusiasm for running for things.

I propose we abandon the pretence entirely and install a monarch.

Before the political science majors begin composing angry letters, let me clarify: I am not advocating for tyranny. I am advocating for efficiency. Thomas Hobbes understood that the natural state of any student body is chaos. We are, by nature, disorganised, self-interested, and perpetually sleep-deprived. We need a sovereign, not to oppress us, but to spare us the annual indignity of pretending we care about senate bylaws.

The selection process would be simple and meritocratic, in the way that all monarchies claim to be. On the first day of the autumn semester, the names of all enrolled students would be placed in a large ceremonial urn, preferably something attractive in brushed bronze that could double as a campus landmark. One name would be drawn at random by the university president, who would serve as the divine intermediary, being the closest thing to God that most students will encounter during their undergraduate careers.

The chosen student would be crowned in a ceremony held in the campus amphitheatre, attended by mandatory participation from all first-year students, who would benefit from the spectacle. The new monarch would receive the following privileges: a reserved parking space, unlimited meal plan credits, a private study room in the library that actually has working outlets, and the authority to allocate the student activities fund without convening a committee.

In exchange, the monarch would be required to hold office hours twice a week, during which any student could petition for funds, lodge complaints, or simply vent about the dining hall. This would be more accessible than the current system, which requires navigating a labyrinth of subcommittees and Google Forms.

Critics will argue that absolute power corrupts absolutely, to which I respond: have you met our current student government treasurer? The corruption is already here. At least with a monarchy, the corruption would be concentrated in a single, easily monitored individual rather than distributed across a byzantine network of caucuses and ad hoc working groups.

The monarchy would serve for one academic year, after which the urn would be brought out again and a new sovereign selected. No re-election campaigns. No transition drama. No LinkedIn posts about leadership journeys. Just a clean transfer of ceremonial power from one randomly selected student to another, which is, when you think about it, roughly how the current system works anyway, minus the Instagram graphics.

I recognise that this proposal will not be popular with the current student government, who have invested considerable time and social capital in the existing framework. To them I say: your service is appreciated, and under the new regime, you will be honoured with titles. Vice President of Communications can become the Royal Herald. The Treasurer can become the Keeper of the Purse. Everyone gets a nicer LinkedIn title, and nobody has to sit through another budget meeting.

Long live the King. Or Queen. Or whoever the urn selects. The urn does not discriminate.
